one_line_definition: The phenomenon where the velocity of elastic (ultrasonic) waves changes with applied or residual stress in a solid.

aliases_seen: TOE constants; l, m, n (Murnaghan); third-order elastic constants
one_line_definition: Three scalar constants (l, m, n) characterizing the cubic term in the strain-energy function of an isotropic solid; govern nonlinear acoustic effects.

aliases_seen: SOE constants; λ and μ; second-order elastic constants
one_line_definition: The two second-order elastic constants (λ = bulk-like, μ = shear modulus) of an isotropic solid entering the classical stress-strain relation.
Classical acoustoelastic equations (Hughes & Kelly 1953) expressing the stress-dependence of the nine principal wave velocities in an isotropic material using Murnaghan TOE constants.
Acoustoelastic theory (Man & Lu 1987) using "universal relations" that relate velocity changes to stress without requiring knowledge of the third-order elastic constants.
Description of deformation using coordinates fixed to the deformed state; in acoustoelasticity, leads to Eulerian TOE constants (l, m, n) distinct from Lagrangian ones.
Description of deformation using material (undeformed) coordinates; Hughes-Kelly and most classical work uses Lagrangian formulation.
A 54-component tensor (Takahashi) connecting stress increments linearly to infinitesimal displacement gradients in the finitely deformed Eulerian frame.
The splitting of an ultrasonic shear wave into two orthogonally polarized components with different velocities when propagating normal to a uniaxial stress.
The time taken for an ultrasonic pulse to travel a known distance; changes with stress via the acoustoelastic effect and provides the primary measurement signal.
The transformation of one ultrasonic wave mode (e.g., shear) into another (e.g., longitudinal) at a surface or interface; exploited in bolt and bearing measurements to avoid length dependency.
A non-contact ultrasonic transducer that generates and receives ultrasonic waves via electromagnetic induction, not requiring coupling fluid.

aliases_seen: surface wave; Rayleigh surface acoustic wave
one_line_definition: An elastic wave that propagates along a free surface with particle motion in an elliptical retrograde orbit; depth penetration ~one wavelength; sensitive to near-surface stress.

aliases_seen: guided plate wave; A0 mode; S0 mode
one_line_definition: Guided elastic waves in a thin plate; A0 = antisymmetric (flexural), S0 = symmetric (extensional) modes; used for large-area SHM.
A flexible piezoelectric polymer (β-phase) used as a broadband ultrasonic sensor; d₃₁ ≈ −37.69×10⁻¹² m/V; no resonance artifacts at high frequency.

aliases_seen: acoustoelastic constant; B; Lzz; K; M
one_line_definition: Material-specific constant relating fractional velocity change to applied stress or strain; depends on SOE and TOE constants.
High-precision TOF measurement where a received echo re-triggers the transmitter; the resulting repetition frequency tracks the transit time; resolution ~1 in 10⁶.
High-precision measurement where the pulse repetition rate is servo-controlled to align pulse superpositions; resolution ~1 in 10⁷.
Phase-based acoustoelastic measurement comparing two echoes from front and back surfaces; used in Kino's imaging system.
Non-random grain orientation in polycrystalline metals from processing (rolling, forging); causes acoustic birefringence that can mask stress-induced birefringence.
Interaction of ultrasonic waves with dislocation networks; affects attenuation and velocity; complicates stress measurement especially after plastic deformation.
Classical model (Granato & Lücke 1956) treating dislocations as vibrating strings pinned at ends; predicts monotonically increasing attenuation with dislocation density.
Statistical model describing hysteresis via a distribution of two-state "hysteron" elements on the Preisach triangle; applied to acoustic velocity hysteresis under cyclic loading.

Stress remaining in a component after external loads are removed; acoustoelastic measurement of residual stress requires that the material's velocity is not permanently altered by prior plastic deformation.
TOE constants measured for plain carbon steels of varying carbon content (Takahashi 1987); n varies with carbon content.
Application of acoustoelasticity to measure the stress distribution across a cross-section under bending (non-uniform, depth-varying stress field).
Signal processing operation computing the analytic signal; used to extract instantaneous phase and remove contact-stiffness-dependent phase shifts in bearing ToF measurements.
The fraction of ultrasonic energy from an incident wave scattered into a given direction by grain boundaries; stress-dependent in polycrystals.
The nine mode-specific scattering coefficients (L→L, L→T, T→L, T→T combinations) governing grain scattering amplitude in a stressed polycrystal.
Departure from Hooke's law due to finite strain; characterized by third- (and higher-) order elastic constants; responsible for harmonic generation and acoustoelastic effects.
Generation of overtone frequencies as a finite-amplitude wave propagates in a nonlinear medium; amplitude governed by TOE constants.
Interaction between elastic wave quanta (phonons) due to lattice anharmonicity; describes the same physics as acoustic harmonic generation from a quantum perspective.
The dependence of ultrasonic scattering amplitude from grain boundaries on material stress state; more sensitive to stress than phase velocity changes.
Acoustoelastic birefringence theory for slightly orthotropic plates retaining anisotropy in second-order but not third-order moduli; gives one acoustoelastic constant.
Acoustoelastic birefringence theory based on an optical refraction-index-matrix analogy; uses three acoustoelastic constants; empirically matches data well.
The tensile stress (or force) in a threaded fastener; measurable ultrasonically via TOF change or amplitude ratio of mode-converted signals.
The normal force transmitted from a rolling element to the raceway; measurable via acoustoelastic TOF change in the raceway material.
The wave propagation equation in a prestressed anisotropic medium; involves stress-dependent elastic constants in the generalized Christoffel tensor.
Use of ultrasonic velocity or attenuation changes to track accumulated fatigue damage; velocity peak at ~20% lifetime and attenuation peak at ~20% are indicators.
Repeated application of torsional stress; used in Serdobolskaya (2002) to study acoustic hysteresis; creates dislocation accumulation.
Non-recoverable change in ultrasonic wave velocity after plastic cycling; forms a characteristic butterfly-shaped loop on velocity-vs-stress diagram.

aliases_seen: Re = ρωu₀λ/η; nonlinear acoustic Mach parameter
one_line_definition: Dimensionless ratio governing second-harmonic amplitude at stabilization distance; Re = ρωu₀λ/η where u₀ is displacement amplitude and η is effective viscosity; (u_x)_max/u₀ ~ Re.
Distance at which the second acoustic harmonic reaches its maximum amplitude before dissipation dominates; x_s = ln2/2αᵢ where αᵢ is the longitudinal attenuation coefficient.
Three-wave acoustic interaction satisfying both energy (ω₁±ω₂=ω₃) and quasimomentum (k₁±k₂=k₃) conservation; leads to continuously growing combination-frequency wave.
Three-wave acoustic interaction NOT satisfying phase-matching; results in a spatially "amplitude-modulated" harmonic with period Δx = λᵢ/2(α−1) ~ λ_T; very small amplitude, no accumulation.

Theory (Landau & Rumer 1937) predicting absorption of high-frequency sound via three-phonon processes with thermal phonons; valid when λ_ac ≪ λ_ph; predicts αᵢ ~ ωT⁴ for transverse waves but underestimates longitudinal absorption.
Ultrasound absorption mechanism (Akhiezer 1939) valid when λ_ac ≫ λ_ph; acoustic wave modulates thermal phonon equilibrium, which relaxes via phonon-phonon interactions; complements LR theory.

Correction to LR theory (Maris 1963, Simons 1963) allowing longitudinal phonons to interact non-exactly collinearly due to finite phonon lifetime; resolves contradiction between LR prediction αᵢ ≪ αᵢ and experiment αᵢ ~ αᵢ.

aliases_seen: light diffraction by ultrasound; acoustic Bragg diffraction
one_line_definition: Method of detecting acoustic harmonics by scanning a laser beam through a crystal at angles satisfying the Bragg condition sin θ_B = λ_light/2Λ_sound for each harmonic; avoids transducer calibration and Raman-Nath artefacts.

Generation of hypersound (~ 10¹⁰ Hz) by focused giant-pulse laser radiation via nonlinear light-matter interaction; at room temperature hypersound power too low for nonlinear effects; at helium temperature can drive sawtooth waveforms implicated in laser damage.

Highly sensitive technique using amplitude-modulated carrier in a high-Q rod resonator; nonlinear elasticity separates the low modulation frequency Ω via acoustic detection; sensitivity Re ~ 0.05 achievable with Q ~ 10⁵.

Third-order elastic constants defined by Brugger (1964) for crystals using full index notation C_{ij,pq,rs}; for cubic crystals (O, Oh, Th) only 6 independent constants; for triclinic crystals up to 56 independent constants.

Truncation of the elastic energy expansion at third order, retaining the two Lamé constants (λ, μ) and three Murnaghan TOE constants (l, m, n); sufficient for second-harmonic generation and low-Reynolds-number nonlinear effects.
